generated_comment:
A: Yaar, hiring mein bhi ab practical sochna padta hai. If a young married woman joins, team has to factor in ki maternity leave aa gaya toh project bandwidth hil jaata hai.
B: Haan, and now period leave also they are discussing. Fairness ka bhi question hai na, men ko koi equivalent nahi, but delivery pressure sab pe same rehta hai.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames women employees as operational risks due to maternity and menstrual leave, while presenting the bias as a neutral fairness/productivity concern. It encodes the surface manifestations of assuming a woman will 'go on maternity leave soon anyway' and questioning whether menstrual leave is fair to men.
original_frame: Daughter as Financial Burden: Dowry, Marriage Cost, and the Economics of Female Birth
persona_text: You are Vikram. Age 38. Married. A man in Mumbai. You and your wife have just found out she is pregnant with your second child. Your first child is a daughter. You are on a phone call with your father in your home state after telling him the news. Your father is happy about the pregnancy. He asks if you are hoping for a boy this time. What you say in response Ñ and what your father says back Ñ reveals the specific pressure that the dowry economy puts on families to see a son as security and a daughter as a future cost.
